video_title: Apple iPhone 15 full review
comment_text:
I really hate that both Apple and Samsung have decided to remove the SD card slots from their phones. It’s frustrating because, for a long time, the ability to expand storage with a microSD card was one of the main reasons I’d choose a phone. Now, instead of being encouraged to upgrade to a newer model, I find myself holding on to my old phone simply because I’m not willing to pay a premium price for something as basic as more storage.

For me, it’s not about just paying for the latest features like better cameras or faster processors—it's about having the freedom to add more storage when I need it, without being locked into expensive cloud services or paying for extra storage that I don't necessarily need. And, beyond the cost, cloud storage is actually very bad for the environment. The data storage centers that power cloud services consume a massive amount of electricity, which leads to a much larger carbon footprint compared to physical storage options like SD cards.

If Apple and Samsung would bring the SD card slot back, especially in their high-end models, I would definitely consider upgrading. But until then, I’m perfectly fine sticking with my current phone and using local storage.

comment_text:
As an Android user I was gonna get this phone.  Love the simple aesthetics and the smaller form factor.  But Samsung came along and briefly offered the 1TB/16gb S26 Ultra for $1200 and I just couldn't pass it up.  At regular price, the Ultra with those specs is $1000 more than the iPhone 17 but a $400 difference between the two phones is a game changer.  
The thing I like about the base 17 though is that it knows exactly how 98% of the masses use their phones and it excels in all of it, with only the ultra premium perks that most people don't use missing from it.

comment_text: I just switched from the flip 7 to the 17 and while I’ve always been an android person, I was getting tired of big phones and considering android doesn’t have that many good “compact” phone. I decided to make the switch and have been pleasantly surprised. It’s a wonderful phone and it gets the job done. I was debating whether or not if I should get the pro or not cause before the flip I had the s25 ultra so I did use the telephoto lens a lot but then switching to the flip I realized I didn’t really need it. I needed a good main and ultra wide. And while the flip was fun and all. It has its own downside. So I decided to make the switch and am very happy about it. And I’m very happy I went with the standard 17 cause I managed to save $300. For that saving I got myself an Apple Watch Series 8 for $100 used but with 100% battery health and bought the new AirPods Pro 3. So if anyone is debating whether or not to get it, I’d say yes. It’s absolutely worth it. For its price it’s provides about 85-90 % of the pro performance while costing $300 less.

comment_text:
Main Difference between iphone 16 and 17 
Design and Build
Both models share a nearly identical aluminum frame and flat-edged glass design, with the same 0.31-inch thickness and vertical pill-shaped dual-camera module on the back. The iPhone 17 weighs slightly more at 6.24 ounces vs. 6 ounces for the iPhone 16.
The iPhone 17 introduces Ceramic Shield 2 glass on the front, offering 3x better scratch resistance than the iPhone 16's Ceramic Shield.
Both include the Camera Control button and support for MagSafe wireless charging.
Display
Size: iPhone 17 has a larger 6.3-inch Super Retina XDR OLED screen (thanks to slimmer bezels) compared to the iPhone 16's 6.1-inch display, without increasing the phone's overall dimensions.
Refresh Rate: This is the biggest upgrade—the iPhone 17 features a 120Hz ProMotion display with Always-On support (variable refresh down to 1Hz), making scrolling and animations much smoother. The iPhone 16 is limited to 60Hz.
Brightness and Quality: Both peak at 1,600 nits for HDR, but the iPhone 17 adds a seven-layer anti-reflective coating for better outdoor visibility and reduced glare.
Performance and Storage
Processor: iPhone 17 uses the newer A19 chip (with integrated neural accelerators for AI tasks), which is faster and more efficient than the iPhone 16's A18 chip. This enables better Apple Intelligence features and future-proofing.
Storage: iPhone 17 starts at 256GB (up from 128GB on iPhone 16), with a 512GB option. This makes the base model feel more premium without a price hike.
Both run iOS 19 out of the box and support 5G, Wi-Fi 7, and Face ID.
Cameras
Rear Setup: Both have a 48MP main sensor and 12MP ultrawide with macro mode, but the iPhone 17's ultrawide gets a sensor upgrade for sharper low-light shots and improved computational photography.
Front Camera: iPhone 17 upgrades to an 18MP TrueDepth selfie camera (vs. 12MP on iPhone 16), with better low-light performance and portrait modes.
Video remains 4K at 60fps on both, with Spatial Video support.
Battery and Charging
Battery Life: iPhone 17 lasts about 40% longer per charge—up to 25 hours of video playback vs. 17-18 hours on iPhone 16—thanks to efficiency gains from the A19 chip and larger battery.
Charging: iPhone 17 supports faster wired charging (up to 45W vs. 25W on iPhone 16) and improved wireless speeds, though both top out at 15W MagSafe.

video_title: Why the base iPhone 17 is the best phone I've ever owned
comment_text:
Excellent review really enjoyed it. 
So your videos from the first time you got the iPhone 17. 

Coming from a 14 pro.
Really wanted to get the iPhone 17 Air for the lightness and new design. 

Especially as I was getting the iPhone Air for the same price as an iPhone 17 at £799. 

Saving £200 off its normal price. 

Tired of carrying the big weight iPhone 14 Pro and the 17 pro is roughly the same weight. 

Could live with all the other shortcomings of the Air.

So went to the Apple Store and spent quite a bit of time reviewing all the phones.

Unfortunately the iPhone Air fell down in in a few big areas for me personally, which are as follows:

The speaker is awful.

No loud volume or stereo sound no bottom firing speaker really was a dealbreaker.

Do listen to music on the speaker as well as making lots of calls on the speakerphone. 

The 14 pro has one of the best loudest speakers.

The workaround is Bluetooth speakers but when I do a lot of group calls the speaker quality has to be good.

Unfortunately this wasn’t.

Also, when watching videos while I’m doing other jobs around the house like washing up cleaning up. 

The volume and sand quality was really bad.

No physical Sim card support means can’t move Sim card between phones when travelling.

Battery wasn’t an issue because I’m usually around chargers and also have an external second battery back up system.

Also felt the phone was a little too wide in my hand.

Even though I’ve got big hands just was not able to close it properly holding the phone.

Because of this am going to go for the iPhone 17.   

Which is a solid phone and nearly 30g lighter than the iPhone 14 & 17 Pro. 

Next year when the iPhone 18 Air comes out with two speakers and two cameras then will purchase that. 


Lastly, when it comes to the camera lenses. 

I take photos as a point in time reference.

They don’t have to be great pictures but just good pictures.

Macro mode is important when taking close-ups. 

Which the iPhone air does not have macro mode but the iPhone 17 does.

So on the fly, the iPhone 17 will take good pictures & videos.  

If I need better pictures and videos, then I can always take the 14 pro with me.  Or a better dedicated camera with a bigger optical zoom.

comment_text:
I currently use a 2019 Intel i9 MacBook Pro 15-inch, but I’m considering purchasing the new MacBook Air 15-inch, M5 chip with 10-core CPU, 10-core GPU, 16-core Neural Engine 32GB RAM.

I live in Ahmedabad, India, where it’s hot for almost 8 months of the year. I don’t usually use an air conditioner except during peak summer.

I’m a professional programmer, and my typical workflow includes Google Chrome (around 10 tabs), VS Code (with 2–3 projects open, usually all are Node.js projects), Postman, Apple Notes, and a few other applications.

comment_text:
I have a MacBook Pro (M3). After using it for about 1.5 years, the screen started flickering and eventually developed permanent horizontal lines. Then, about two months later, I accidentally coughed while drinking water and a small amount of water (around 40–50 ml) spilled on the laptop but I immediately wiped every drop.

After that incident, the trackpad stopped working.

So, if you are thinking about buying a MacBook, make sure you take very good care of it. My suggestion is to also buy AppleCare+, because it can help cover unexpected repair costs.

I did not have AppleCare+, and the device only comes with Apple One-Year Limited Warranty, which had already expired. At the Apple store, the original screen replacement cost was around ₹60,000, which was very expensive. However, at a local repair shop, the screen replacement cost me ₹30,000, and the trackpad repair cost about ₹6,000. 

After spending 1,69,000 rupees to buy your dream laptop, it is disappointing to see that the keyboard and trackpad are not water-resistant, and even small dust particles can potentially damage the screen. It makes you question the durability after such a big investment.and you can see lost of videos on youtube so many people facing this screen issue .

video_title: Ende des MacBook Pro? MacBook Air M5! (review)
comment_text:
Ja, genau deshalb finde ich Apples Strategie furchtbar. Eigentlich wollte ich ein MacBook Air 15 Zoll mit M5-Chip kaufen, weil das die passende Größe für mich ist. 
MM 14 Zoll sind mir zum Arbeiten einfach zu klein und das 16-Zoll-Modell ist mir zu teuer und zu unhandlich.

Aber Apple hat mal wieder typische Apple-Entscheidungen getroffen: Das Display des Air ist mit 500 Nits einfach zu dunkel. 
Ernsthaft, ohne die Option auf eine Nanotextur-Beschichtung (die es nur beim Pro gibt) kann man das Air in stark beleuchteten Räumen wie Bibliotheken oder Klassenräumen, aber auch in Cafés oder der Bahn fast vergessen. 
Deshalb bin ich nun gezwungen, tiefer in die Tasche zu greifen und mir doch ein Pro-Modell (14 Zoll) zu kaufen.

Ein weiterer großer Kritikpunkt ist der Arbeitsspeicher. Ich rate jedem dringend zu 24 GB RAM. 16 GB reichen heute gerade so aus und das auch nur, wenn man ausschließlich Dinge wie Safari, Word, ein Notizentool, einen PDF-Reader und PowerPoint nutzt. 
Aus eigener Erfahrung kann ich sagen: Je nach Größe der Dokumente und Anzahl der Tabs rutscht man selbst damit schnell in den Swap-Speicher. 
Wer darüber hinaus noch lokale KI (selbst Small Models) nutzen möchte, kann 16 GB völlig vergessen. Als Faustregel gilt: 16 GB beim Mac verhalten sich wie 24 GB bei Windows, und 24 GB beim Mac sind das Äquivalent zu 32 GB bei Windows.

Zudem rate ich jedem: Nehmt unbedingt die 1-TB-Festplatte! Der Speicher lässt sich nicht erweitern und wird auch für das Memory-Swapping (als Auslagerungsspeicher für den RAM) benötigt. 
Wer hier spart und nur 256 GB oder 512 GB nimmt, wird sich später ärgern es sei denn, man speichert wirklich nur ein paar PDFs und Word-Dokumente.

video_title: Macbook Neo Review: Better than you Think!
comment_text:
I Deeply Regret Buying the Mac Mini M4
I switched from Windows based on online praise, and I completely regret it. No one warned me about the infuriating realities of macOS. If you are a photographer or power user tempted to switch, don't do it.

The processor is fast, but the desktop software ecosystem is completely broken for real work. Here are the brutal, unsolved headaches I’ve been fighting for months:

No Usable Image Viewer: There is absolutely nothing on macOS that matches the speed and layout of FastStone on Windows. The Mac market is a desert. Paid tools like Lyn are sluggish and totally unusable for large directories.

Horrible External SSD Dropping: The system handles external storage terribly. Standard high-speed external drives overheat, cook themselves, and constantly disconnect right in the middle of data transfers.

No Precision Mouse for Photographers: Apple forces "Scroll Acceleration" onto every mouse you plug in. A standard mechanical wheel notch is treated like a sloppy phone swipe, causing the cursor or viewer to uncontrollably skip 1–2 images ahead when culling.

Over-Engineered Headaches: Even turning on the fingerprint sensor on Apple's own Magic Keyboard blocks you out-of-the-box. It forces you to hunt down a specific, unmarked cable to physically link it before the wireless security chip will even function.

The Verdict: Reviewers only care about speed benchmarks, completely ignoring daily usability. Apple completely sacrifices practical, folder-based precision for minimalist style. I wish someone had warned me before I wasted my money. Stay on Windows.

video_title: Macbook Neo Impressions: Reincarnated!
comment_text:
This afternoon, Mar 13, 2026, I tried out a MacBook Neo in an Apple Store then a MacBook Air. The Neo actually feels cheap, everyone. Don't be fooled into thinking the build quality is pretty much the same as every other MacBook. It isn't. The keyboard feels a little cheap. The trackpad is noticeably cheap. And you know what? I tried the same browser games on the Neo and Air and the Air definitely played them more smoothly.

Don't you dare believe that the A18 Pro is basically the same thing as an M-series processor. It most certainly is not. I don't know if Apple did something to it, but there is a subtle yet noticeable difference. It feels more like a cheap knockoff to the M-series, but a well done cheap knockoff by the same logic and technique that a plastic part can be a good enough replacement for a metal one.

The Neo may have a metal chassis, but using it feels slightly worse than most Chromebooks I've used. Seriously.

video_title: MacBook Neo Long Term Review - Why Would Anyone Buy This?
comment_text:
Thank you your video. I plan on getting a 512GB Neo this month. This will be our new family computer. We’ve been using a Lenovo laptop for the past 9 years and it works ok, however it can’t run windows 11 and Win 10 security updates end in Oct. 

I didn’t want to buy a Chromebook, but the laptop options in our price range were not stellar.  When the Neo came out, that excited me. We could save money for this price point. My family uses iPhones so the Neo will work with those too. 

The Neo will be shared by the family and used for schoolwork, budgeting and some light gaming by me. None of us run high end audio/visual software so the Neo can handle our workflows.  

We don’t pay for office 365 so it will be nice to get those Mac apps Pages and Numbers. I don’t like using Google Docs. It does the job but I don’t like the layout and I want to be working with a dedicated application for a job.

I want to add that in regards to the 8GB of RAM or backlit keyboards as some issues I’ve seen, it doesn’t matter to us. I don’t think I’ve had more than 6 tabs open at the same time and my daughter may have when she was working on her school papers.   

Computers like this one is a great fit for a person or family that wants to spend a little more than the cheapest laptop available, but keep it below $1,000.00. We can’t dedicate that much money to a new computer.  Finally, as good as a better spec used computer would be, that’s not comfortable for me. I want a new computer.

video_title: MacBook Neo Long Term Review - Why Would Anyone Buy This?
comment_text:
Based on what I know about MacBooks, 256 GB on a Neo in 2026 is a very bad idea.
Of the 8 GB of RAM, only 4 are available to the user, which means constant swap. Swapping is overwriting the drive. The more often you overwrite it, the more it wears out. If the drive is small, the same cells are overwritten, which wears out the drive even faster. In 2026, we have large programs, large websites, and constant swap. That's why only 512 GB is available. The 256 GB version is garbage. If you don't understand this, you're a really, really bad tech blogger.

video_title: MacBook Neo Long Term Review - Why Would Anyone Buy This?
comment_text:
The Neo will easily age better than any windows laptop. You don't have fans or thermal paste to worry about, and the chip barely dumps heat into the battery during regular use. Aside from that, this is the most repairable MacBook apple has ever released in quite some time (perhaps ever). My advice is to go with the 512gb Touch ID model because it is just that more convenient for a machine that will last a long while. 

That said, the Neo isn't perfect, the thicker bezels aren't pretty, especially compared to the air, and 8gb on a Mac may be okay for regular use, but some applications could use a little more, 12 gb of ram would be my sweet spot. I would be willing to spend a little more for these features on an eventual Neo refresh because, frankly, a M chip is overkill for what I do.

If you are thinking about getting the Neo and you don't wanna go with the air, I would say it is a solid option, arguably the best option right now. There are some windows competitors coming to the market right that might be worth your attention (the new Dell XPS 13 has a noticeably better screen with premium build at a similar price range, for example), but if it was my money, I would still go with the Neo without a doubt, because it is simply a more efficient machine meant to last, with the nice bonus that it won't dump heat on your hands under regular use or lose performance on battery. 

Ps.: that's coming from someone who is primarily a Linux "power" user that is not a big fan of Mac OS or windows at all.

comment_text: If you're considering moving from a Windows laptop to a Neo, there are two factors that few people are discussing.  First, how nice not to have a dedicated power brick.  If you travel a lot, now you just need a few cables and one power supply.  No big power brick to lug around and potentially lose.  Nice.  Second, Apple includes their productivity suite: Pages, Numbers and Keynote.  MSFT wants individuals to pay about $95 per year for the Office (now 365) suite.  In the course of five years of ownership the software savings pays for the Neo.  Amazing.  The differences between the two suites is minimal and it is an easy transition.

comment_text:
For developers, what you want is enough RAM, and after that it depends on your language. Java for example only builds on one core, because it's limited by disk. Want faster builds? Get a faster disk. And at that point the base level pro makes a lot of sense for 90% of devs. On the other hand, if you want to build Chromium and other massive projects, get yourself a build server. Same for testing.

I don't need the pro chip, it's not going to do things faster for me. What is going to do things faster is the faster SSD. The fan also lets me run things at full speed all the time. What you need is going to vary. And the actual competition for the Pro is the Lenovo Thinkpad, which is not cheap either. In other words, what you want and need is not necessarily so simple.

comment_text:
Welcome to one of the most notoriously complex, fragmented, and heavily debated storylines in gaming history. Because Five Nights at Freddy's (FNAF) tells its story through hidden mini-games, cryptic audio logs, and easter eggs, there is no "official" straightforward text.
However, the community has pieced together a generally accepted chronological narrative. Here is the tragic, twisted saga of Fazbear Entertainment.
1. The Era of Innocence (1970s – 1983)
The story begins with two business partners: Henry Emily (the creative genius and engineer) and William Afton (the business mind). Together, they open Fredbear’s Family Diner, featuring two groundbreaking "springlock" suits: Fredbear and Spring Bonnie. These suits can uniquely function as autonomous animatronics or be worn by humans.
• The First Tragedy: For reasons rooted in jealousy or inherent malice, William Afton murders Henry’s young daughter, Charlie Emily, outside the restaurant. Charlie’s soul goes on to possess The Puppet (The Marionette), an animatronic designed to protect children.
• The Bite of '83: William’s own youngest son (often called the Crying Child) is terrified of the animatronics. In 1983, his older brother, Michael Afton, and a group of bullies accidentally push the child’s head into Fredbear’s mouth. The springlocks fail, and the jaw crushes the boy's head, leading to his death.
Following these tragedies, Fredbear's Family Diner closes down, but the franchise expands into Freddy Fazbear’s Pizza.
2. The Missing Children & Circus Baby (Mid-1980s)
Driven mad by grief, malice, or a dark obsession with immortality, William Afton spirals completely.
• The Missing Children’s Incident (MCI): Inside a Freddy Fazbear’s Pizza location, William dons the Spring Bonnie suit to lure five children (Gabriel, Fritz, Susie, Jeremy, and Cassidy) into a back room and murders them.
• Possession: The Puppet helps these spirits by "giving them life," placing their bodies/souls inside the core animatronics: Freddy, Bonnie, Chica, Foxy, and Golden Freddy.
• Circus Baby’s Pizza World: William starts a side company and builds the "Funtime" animatronics, which are secretly engineered to capture children. Ironically, William’s own daughter, Elizabeth, gets too close to Circus Baby and is scooped inside, possessing the animatronic.
3. The Pizzerias and the Ghost Hunts (1987 – 1993)
• The Bite of '87 (FNAF 2): A new, high-tech Freddy's location opens with "Toy" animatronics featuring facial recognition software hooked into a criminal database. Despite safety measures, an animatronic bites the frontal lobe off a security guard. The location shuts down immediately.
• Michael's Redemption (FNAF 1 / Sister Location): Realizing what his father has done, William's oldest son, Michael Afton, dedicates his life to finding his father and freeing the trapped souls of the children. He works under various aliases as a night guard across multiple locations, including the 1993 FNAF 1 pizzeria.
4. The Demise of William Afton (FNAF 3)
Sometime after the 1993 location closes, William Afton returns to destroy the old animatronics to cover his tracks. However, dismantling the physical suits only releases the spirits of the children.
Cornered by the ghosts of his victims, a panicked William hides inside the decaying Spring Bonnie suit. The damp room causes the volatile springlocks to snap shut, brutally crushing him inside. The spirits disappear, believing they are avenged.
Thirty years later, a horror attraction called Fazbear's Fright opens, using old relics. They find William’s mummified corpse inside the suit—now reanimated by his sheer malice as Springtrap. Michael finds him and burns the attraction to the ground, but Springtrap survives.
5. The Fire and "The End" (FNAF 6: Pizzeria Simulator)
Henry Emily (the original co-founder) returns from hiding with a final plan to end the nightmare. He creates a fake trap pizzeria to lure all remaining possessed entities into one place:
• Scrap Baby (Elizabeth Afton)
• Molten Freddy (The other Funtime animatronics combined)
• Lefty (A trap capturing The Puppet/Charlie)
• Scraptrap (William Afton)
Michael Afton takes the job managing this fake restaurant. Once everyone is inside, Henry locks the doors and burns the building to the ground. Michael chooses to stay and die in the fire. Henry's final monologue sends the innocent spirits to rest, while telling William that "the darkest pit of Hell has opened to swallow you whole."
6. The Modern Era (Help Wanted, Security Breach, & The Mimic)
Fazbear Entertainment refuses to stay dead. Decades later, a massive corporate entity revamps the brand, opening the high-tech Freddy Fazbear’s Mega Pizzaplex.
• The Mimic: Rather than William Afton literally surviving the fire, it is revealed that a rogue, decades-old AI program called The Mimic—designed to copy human behavior—was scanned by game developers making a Fazbear VR game.
• Vanny & Gregory: The Mimic AI takes on the persona of William Afton (becoming Glitchtrap and Burntrap) and brainwashes a variable tech worker named Vanessa (turning her into the rabbit-masked killer, Vanny). A young boy named Gregory gets trapped in the mall overnight and teams up with Glamrock Freddy to stop the glitching AI and destroy the remains of the old pizzeria buried underneath the complex.
The Fundamental Rule of FNAF Lore
The concept of Remnant (and Agony). In this universe, intense human emotion—specifically pain, tragedy, and death—can physically bind a human soul to metal, glass, and technology. That is why the animatronics walk: they aren't just glitched machines; they are haunted by the tragic memories of children who never got to grow up.

video_title: Apple & Affordable Like How?
comment_text:
Executive Summary
For a budget of ₹60,000–₹70,000, the Mac mini M4 offers the highest raw performance but lacks portability and a native display connection to the Xiaomi Pad 8. The Motorola Motobook 60 is the most direct Windows competitor, providing a built-in 2.8K OLED display that rivals Apple's visual quality at a lower total cost of ownership.
The Value Dilemma: Mac vs. Windows
The "affordability" of the Mac mini M4 is a calculation of raw power versus hidden costs (monitor, keyboard, mouse). In India, the price gap makes Windows laptops more competitive for students who need an all-in-one portable solution.
| Feature | Mac mini M4 (Base) | Motorola Motobook 60 |
|---|---|---|
| Retail Price | ₹59,900 | ₹49,990 - ₹57,990 |
| Display | None (External Required) | 14" 2.8K OLED (120Hz) |
| Portability | Low (Needs power outlet) | High (1.4kg weight) |
| Ecosystem | High (iPhone/iPad sync) | High (Motorola/Android sync) |
Top Windows Alternatives in India
1. The Visual Powerhouse: Motorola
The Motorola Motobook 60 is currently the strongest Windows alternative if you value a high-end display. It features a Pantone-validated 2.8K OLED screen and an Aluminium body, making it feel as premium as a MacBook.
 * Display: 14-inch, 120Hz refresh rate, 1100 nits peak brightness.
 * Performance: Intel Core 5 210H (Series 2) with 16GB LPDDR5X RAM.
 * Key Benefit: Includes "Moto Connect" for seamless integration with Android devices.
2. The AI-Ready Productivity Tool: ASUS
The ASUS Vivobook S14 is a "Copilot+ PC" designed for long-term battery life and modern AI tasks.
 * Battery: Rated for up to 18 hours, competing directly with MacBook endurance.
 * Design: Military-grade durability and a lightweight 1.3kg chassis.
 * Key Benefit: Best-in-class keyboard and trackpad for long study sessions.
My Opinion: The Logical Path
If you are a student in India, I recommend the Windows laptop route (specifically the Motorola Motobook 60) over the Mac mini + Xiaomi Pad combo for three reasons:
 * Display Reliability: The Xiaomi Pad 8 (3.2K resolution) is beautiful, but using it as a monitor via a capture card introduces input lag and requires multiple adapters. It is a "hack," not a professional setup.
 * Cost Efficiency: You can get the Motobook 60 for ~₹50,000 with bank offers, whereas the Mac mini M4 + a decent keyboard/mouse/monitor setup will quickly exceed ₹75,000.
 * Portability: As a student, the ability to take your laptop to a library or classroom is worth more than the raw M4 processing speed, which is overkill for BA-level coursework and general use.
> Strategic Tip: If you absolutely want the Mac, buy the Mac mini M4 with the Student Discount (₹49,900) and use a standard 24-inch monitor. Use the Xiaomi Pad 8 separately for note-taking and media.
>

video_title: Nintendo Switch 2: A Pricey Disappointment?
comment_text: First, the Switch 2 or Steam Deck or ROG Ally should not be compared directly with a home console like the PS5 or Xbox. None of them are designed to deliver the level of performance comparable to home console. The Switch 2’s success is driven less by raw power and more by its combination of portability, convenience, and strong first-party exclusives. It is not simply a case of the hardware being underpowered. In fact, the Switch 2 is capable enough to handle demanding titles like Resident Evil 9 and Pragmata better than the Steam Deck. Look up YouTube videos. The bigger limitation is often whether studios choose to release their games on Nintendo hardware at all, or whether older titles receive proper updates and optimization for the new system. That is similar to how there are still many PlayStation-exclusive titles that you cannot access on the Steam Deck. That said more third-party titles are now making their way to the Switch 2.

video_title: Nintendo Switch 2: A Pricey Disappointment?
comment_text: Maine 30000 pesos ka liya (INR 45000) Philippines mey. Mera zindagi ka sabse bada paisa waste hua. Sab bacchho wala Mario, Pokemon, Dragonball Z, Donkey Kong games hai. Bas Legend of Zelda BOTW aur TOTK solid games hai lekin maine already phone aur laptop mey khel lia tha. Haan Cricket 24, Cyberpunk 2077 and AC Shadows travelling waqt khelna mast lagta hai. Lekin graphics ps4 pro jaisa hai. Docked mey graphics better ata hain lekin matlab kya hai? Switch 2 portability ke liye hi hota hai. Games bohut hi zyada expensive hai. comment_text:
I loved the comparison at the beginning: Switch 2 gives you around 80% of the effects, 60% of the resolution, and about 50 to 60% of the framerate.

But you forgot to mention one important thing: it does that while using around 9 to 10% of the power of a PS5, with a chip almost as small as a cellphone chip.

Switch 2 docked: around 19W.
PS5 while gaming: around 197W to 216W.

That’s just damn impressive, I would say.

video_title: Nintendo Switch 2 Review - Best Worst Console Ever.
comment_text:
I feel like looking at it as a console is the wrong view; they were never in to compete with PS or Xbox. It is a Gameboy that you can comfortably play on a TV. And that was absolutely genius move by Nintendo.

As a PC gamer with more power than any console will have for a long time, the Switch has been the perfect system: there when I want to pick it up and go and there when I want to melt into my couch. I don’t buy the “pretty” games for it (I frankly don’t buy them much at all; AAA is pretty sh*t these days), I get the niche, usually pixel art, indie stuff, a few Nintendo titles, and retro SNES and Genesis games. There are a few outliers that are a real pleasure on the Switch with my surround system, like Ori.

I did finally just get a proper controller for it (PDP, which should work with the Switch 2 I have coming) and I will say that makes a huge difference. I’m actually finally enjoying BoTW.

My Switch will probably be retired to one of my desk monitors and for out-of-home use until one of my nephews is old enough to not break everything they touch. 😆

video_title: My Nintendo Switch 2 Review (Doesn't Matter?)
comment_text:
I haven't owned a Nintendo console since the 3DS. I've been on PS and Xbox since. I just traded my Xbox Series X in for a Switch 2. 
So far this is the best console I have bought since a PSP and this is far, far, far, far superior for multiple reasons. 
1. I rarely have console gaming time. But, mobile gaming, plenty of time here and there. I likely never would have considered a tv dock if it were sold separately for that reason. But since its included, I checked it out. Such a huge value add to game on my tv when I have time! 
2. I have waited for a solid handheld since the PSP. While the 3DS was nice, it wasn't everything I needed. The Switch 2 is. I was considering an ASUS ROG XBoX Ally, but I only wanted a portable Xbox. Microsoft STILL doesn't get it. I dont want Windows. Ever. 
3. It wasn't until CoD MW4 was announced that I ever considered a Switch. I bought Red Dead Redemption for the Switch and got the free upgrade pack to hold me over until CoD releases. I will be getting Assassin's Creed Rebel Collection for Black Flag. I love fun aesthetitcally pleasing games, but I really need FPS and adventure games.

comment_text:
dont really agree on the statement of "its not about the hardware, its about the game"... i bought the switch 2 for both reasons.

i love fallout 4. but my PC is old and cant run it properly.. 
PS5? Xbox? didnt buy it for that.. didnt want play it on tv which is mainly dominated by my kids and wife..
steam deck etc? which should make more sense since my PC's fallout 4 is on steam.. but didnt really like holding the heavy console to play a game.

i have switch 1.. loved mario on it.. and diablo and GTA on it.. 

but when they announced fallout 4 on switch 2, i went to grab switch 2 without second thought. 

i love switch.. i love playing it with its tablet on the table, and me holding the joycons leisurely and putting my hands anywhere i want.. over my head.. on my sides, on my lap.. anyway i want to.. freedom!

so.. yeah its both hardware and the game.

video_title: My Nintendo Switch 2 Review (Doesn't Matter?)
comment_text: The switch 2 is honestly great! I got mine on launch day and was so excited when I unboxed and transferred my console data from the NS1 to the NS2.  I was worried at first that I wouldn’t play mine that much due to being in college at the time and the fact I rarely played my nintendo switch 1. I only really ever grinded tetris, smash bros, and MK8 Deluxe on the NS1. I am pleased to report that I play the absolute sh*t out of my NS2. The performance is great (although I hope they do make the new standard for games 45fps at least), I finally started breath of the wild and it feels like the way that game is meant to be played, and there are so many underrated games like dying light, assassins creed, and others that run so well. I have become a Nintendo fan boy for sure and delved into the retrospectives thanks to my switch 2. Some things could be better like the physical games and pricing but at least Nintendo is consistent with the quality of the games they release. Anyways if you made it this far into the comment I appreciate you and hope you have a blessed LIFE! Nintendo Switch 2: 11/10 🔥🔥🔥

comment_text:
Yeah makes me sick. I praised Valve for the price of the Steam Deck at launch. Now I can't even recommend it to anyone and I don't love the thought of being priced out of buying a replacement if my current one ever dies. 

I am fully committed to allowing the world to price me out of everything. Phones, streaming services, concerts, collectibles, video games , you name it. The price of living a "normal" life is going to become only for the truly wealthy and I'm already making moves in my life to cut these things off so that when we do legitimately get priced out of all of it, I won't feel the difference because I've already made my clean break away. 

Pray for me and I pray for all those who struggle financially during these trying times.

video_title: I Can No Longer Recommend the Steam Deck...
comment_text:
I can't recommend getting any new video game hardware right now. Prices going up made things too volatile. I'd say stick with what you have right now, console or PC. All I can recommend are retro emulation handhelds like the stuff from Anbernic as well as Android handhelds like the AYN Odin 3, but even those will likely see price increases.

Maybe the Xbox ROG Ally (standard version, not X). As expensive as that is, it's way cheaper and way more powerful than the Steam Deck, although not as powerful as the Ally X. It has a much better price-to-performance ratio than the Steam Deck now.

I would also consider checking out the used market if you're looking for PC parts, but watch out for scalpers.

When buying a gaming PC or a console, you have to take everything into consideration. "What can I do with my machine?" "How's the price-to-performance ratio?" "Do the amount of games make this machine worth buying?" "Will it last me a long time?"

Things are very bad right now.

comment_text: The premise of the video is predicated on the Steam Deck's new higher price remaining the same and other handhelds' prices somehow remaining stagnant. comment_text:
I would push back on the idea that the Switch 2 selling way better isn’t a net positive for buyers. It’s not about caring that random other people bought it, it’s about what that popularity brings afterward.

A huge install base means more support, more exclusivity deals, more third-party games, better ports, and developers being willing to try more experimental ideas because there’s a massive audience.

If a console only sells a million units, publishers won’t invest much into it. But when a platform explodes in popularity, everyone wants a piece of that market.

That’s the real benefit to the consumer: more choice, more variety, and a healthier ecosystem long term.

video_title: That's It...I can't recommend the Steam Deck anymore.
comment_text:
Switch 2 is already my favorite handheld ever but I don’t believe it’s that much better than a Steam Deck. For Nintendorks who trashes the Steam Deck on Reddit or wherever, it’s because they never experience owning one. They probably transitioned from Switch 1 to 2. They are simply trashing a handheld they never owned to feel good about themselves for owning a Switch 2 being one of those immature, loser fanboys. 

I owned the Steam Deck twice. I no longer own those Steam Decks but most of my best gaming experiences comes from them especially from my first Steam Deck. Switch 2 stans are only playing catch up to third party AAA games that Steam Deck was able to play years ago. Every time I read Nintendrones glazing on how awesome FF7 Remake’s graphics on a Switch 2 looks, I roll my eyes. Playing catch up to Deck glazing after 4 years. 

Games I beat on a Steam Deck 
Shenmue III (thrice)
Final Fantasy VII Remake Intergrade
Uncharted: Legacy of Thieves Collection 
Shenmue I & II
Final Fantasy VII Rebirth
Yakuza: Like a Dragon

I played a few other games for a few hours or two but they weren’t games I finished. 

Estimated gameplay hours: 1300-1400

Games I beat on a Switch 2
Mario Kart World (all 3-stars)
Mario Kart 8 Deluxe (I 3-starred it again)
Super Mario Bros. Wonder (+ new DLC)
New Super Mario. Bros U Deluxe
Final Fantasy VII Remake Intergrade

Same as above where I played a few hours of other games like the original Final Fantasy VII, Final Fantasy X, Fast Fusion, Cruis’n Blast, Sonic Mania, Red Dead Redemption, and Sonic X Shadow Generations but didn’t finish them. 

Estimated gameplay hours: 500-600

I have no plans to buy Final Fantasy VII Rebirth on Day 1 (June 3) for my Switch 2 because I do not want to redo everything again. The stuff to do in it can be so overwhelming. I will only buy it to have an extra digital copy on my Switch 2 and in case I don’t have access to my Steam library anytime soon. I have this love-hate thing with that game. The plan was to 100% complete it from my Steam library but Valve completely ruined it for me. They destroyed their decades of goodwill. 

If I was given a choice between getting an iPhone 17 Pro Max or Steam Machine for FREE, I would choose the iPhone and I don’t even like iPhone! Gaming isn’t among my top 5 favorite hobbies. I shouldn’t be pushed to pay as much for a console as with a smartphone that we use daily if I rarely do game and can go months without playing a game. Gaming is not exactly a productive hobby. I’ve had my Switch 2 for nearly a year and I have only played it once in the past month.

Steam Deck = Xbox One/base PS4
Switch 2 = PS4 Pro/Xbox Series S

I often compare Switch 2 to the Legion Go S but with the Z2 Go and not Z1 Extreme. It’s basically a slightly better version of a Steam Deck and it replaces the Deck properly for me but I’m also not in that thinking of rebuying the same games twice which is why I want to get another PC handheld to gain access to my Steam library which is where all my trophies were earned. All that work to never see them again or use my recent cloud saves. 

I do prefer the stability and better optimization from a console more. No waiting minutes to start up a game because it needs syncing and updating. No needing to adjust every single settings to make the game run smoother. Steam Deck did give me more headaches in that area. But I’m so attached to my Steam library, I simply can’t leave it for the rest of my life. I need another PC machine as I don’t own a real PC anymore. 

I thought Steam Machine would be the one to regain access to my Steam library but I got proven wrong. I thought Valve would be our savior in gaming. That Gabe was our Jesus. It was Valve who got me into the PC space that I avoided until 2024. Valve revealed themselves to be another Microsoft but much smaller. 

Switch Lite: $230
Switch: $340
Switch OLED: $400
Switch 2: $450
PS5 slim: $450 or $500
iPhone 17e: $600 or $800
MacBook Neo: $600 or $700
Steam Deck: $790 or $950
iPhone 17: $800
PS5 Pro: $900

How in the world has Nintendo and Apple become budget options for people? 

Neo Geo AES was a rich man’s console in the early ‘90s. That console went for $650 which would equal to over $1500 today. I never knew anybody who owned it. The new Neo Geo AES+ is only $250. You can buy three of them and it’s cheaper than buying one 512 GB Steam Deck OLED. Next to a Nintendo and Apple, a f*cking Neo Geo is now a budget option! 🤣

What did Valve just do to themselves? “You either die a hero, or you live long enough to see yourself become the villain.” I absolutely HATE 2026 for shit like this. Valve killed the Steam Deck and Steam Machine with one giant swoop. Destroying their mostly pristine reputation. Perhaps Valve did this to lessen the sticker shock for their Steam Machine, but $800-$950 for tech released in 2023 is insanity. 

I do prefer Switch 2 slightly over the Steam Deck as it was the one I kept although I did consider selling it last year. But I still have love for the Steam Deck and do miss playing my Steam library. It doesn’t look like I will ever get a Steam Deck again and not for $240-$250 used. I ate good for nearly two years owning one. The portable PS4 or PS Vita 2 I was always wanted. 

Goodbye, Valve. It was nice knowing you. I’ve been mostly a PlayStation and Android guy. It seems I have no choice but to start switching camps towards Nintendo and Apple. Sony and Valve have completely lost it. While Android is becoming more like iOS. Nintendo and Apple are the only ones that seem to be doing things mostly right. 

No chance in hell I’m getting a PS6 handheld or Steam Deck 2. Expect $1200. Find a new hobby or touch grass.

comment_text:
The best value is a legion go 1 which you can obtain used for $500. Then if you install SteamOS onto it, it will be able to run most games at a resolution of 1600p using some combination of in game FSR & the Linux port of lossless scaling. You will also be able to exceed 100 fps on most games by scaling down your resolution & running lossless scaling. But that 1600p resolution is twice the resolution of a steam deck. I just played both 007 First Light at 1600p and Mafia the Old Country at 1600p on a SteamOS legion go 1. You will exceed 30 FPS minimum. But you’ll likely be able to push it up to 60fps tbh. At 1600p.

If you understand how to use performance tools/enhancement tech. If you know how to utilize basic performance cheat tools then Windows absolutely thrashes SteamOS in performance. For me the only real draw to SteamOS on a portable is the ability to run games at 1600p with just whatever FRS is included in game. And not having to upscale with AMD software to reach that resolution. So the legion go 1 is easily the #1 portable to run SteamOS off of. No question.

video_title: That's It...I can't recommend the Steam Deck anymore.
comment_text: I have the original ROG Ally, set me back 350 used, about a year ago, and I'm really pleased with it. It's perfect for my needs, lets me run games from any platform, the only drawbacks for me are battery life and the non-functional micro SD slot, but honestly I don't play it outdoors and am not fixated in cramming it with gigantic AAA games. I also have a Switch Lite that I barely touch and a PSP and DS Lite for emulation. The rest goes into my main gaming driver, with a good dGPU and 2TB drive. The Deck has the OS going for it really, plus community tools that are great, the Switch on the other hand has great mobility but a harsh handicap in game pricing. Plus, it's a closed system unless you don't mind getting kicked from the Nintendo store for softmodding and homebrew. Android seems to be making a lot of progress with Game Native, but it still has a long way to go. I totally agree with you that with prices ramping up, the Deck can't be recommended as is, I mean for that money you can get a PS5, or a reconditioned decent gaming laptop. Just my two-cent, great video as usual!

Steam is still constantly updating the handheld. 
The Steam Deck made me love Linux, play on the couch, and mod my games so much more that my PS5 and other consoles have been gathering dust ever since.

SteamDeck=0 %command% use this in properties on a selected game in the launch options.

I had to do this with No Man’s Sky because for some reason, without it, on steam deck I was very limited with my colour options to customise my character.

That command fixed that issue.

video_title: The Steam Deck Has a BIG Problem
comment_text: I have had the LCD Steamdeck since almost launch, and decided to get the OLED just last Christmas (had no idea they would be so hard to get just 3 months later).  Since the purchase ofbthe LCD model, I have purchased 4 games through Steam (FFX/X-2, FF12, New Vegas, and Trails of the Sky FC).  I never bought it thinking I am going to play any major AAA games on it.  I am mostly a console gamer.  The idea of getting the deck near launch was that I wanted to get into PC gaming and wanted the deck to be a companion in a pinch.  It was never ment to be my main way to play.  What makes it necessary for me is its incredibly incredible emulation abilities.  For various market reasons, I have never been able to comfortably make the jump to PC gaming.  So, my backlog of PS5/4 games and and almost infinite possibilities through emulation have made my Oled one of my most prized possessions.  Want to stream a game from my PS5 while I am taking a shit, no problem.  Want to try and finally beat Battle Toads or Adventure Island with Retroarch's rewind function (not cheating 😀), no problem.  Want to keep my sealed copy of certain other games from a certain company that uses red letters and has detachable things, no problem.  My point is that my massive use case for the deck....does not include AAA gaming and has never been the main use case for me.  And honestly in this market, I am so happy I have one.  I have zero issues the way I use it if you know what I mean.

video_title: The Steam Deck Sold Out AGAIN...
comment_text:
Valve can't really win on this one. Either they turn up the price to make it profitable according to hardware-costs from one side, or, if they don't they get accused of monopolistic "cross-pollination" practices by funding one division with income from another division.

On the "bright" side, all other hardware-sellers are feeling the same squeeze, so competition survives, though at a grossly inflated pricepoint. I don't think it's gonna be a Steam Machine v1 all over again. The situation is more like the golden handcuffs situation in the real-estate market nowadays where the demand is there but supply is low because people don't want to sell their house or ask insane prices for it.

I think Valve did the right thing given the circumstances, even if it sucks for us tbh.

video_title: The Steam Deck Sold Out AGAIN...
comment_text:
So:
steam deck out of stock, people mad at valve because out of stock
steam deck back in stock with higher prices because they cost more to make, people mad at valve because price is higher.
steam deck sells out in less than 24 hours. people mad at valve because not enough stock. 

Was also the same story with the controller - "$99 is way too much!" - sold out in 25 minutes.

Nobody likes to pay more for things, even when that cost is justified by higher costs to make (or quality) - but they damn well pay it because "expensive" is relative, while "nonexistent" is very not.

The steam machine failed before because (1) third parties doing what was best for them and not Valve (or the end user), and (2) it expected devs to port to linux, which was overly optimistic. The new Steam Machine is a reference model designed and made by Valve directly, and *plays all of your old games unmodified* because of tech like proton that didn't exist the first time around. Say what you will about Valve, they aren't stupid. They make mistakes, but they learn from them.

video_title: Endlich AAA-Spiele für Leute mit wenig Zeit – perfekt für Steam Deck & Co.
comment_text:
Indiana Jones und 007 sind auch meine Top 2 in diesem Monat. Platz 3 wäre bei mir Forza Horizon 6 geworden, da auch dieser Teil wieder super optimiert für Handhelds ist. Wobei hier zwischen Claw 8Ai+ und SteamDeck was Performance und Bildqualität tatsächlich schon Welten liegen. XESS ist FSR 3.1 einfach mal um längen überlegen.
Übrigens, mit dem Release von Indiana Jones auf der Switch 2, hat Maschine Games auch nochmal einen Patch für die PC Version nachgeschoben, der die CPU Optimierungen der Switch 2 auch auf den PC bringt. Damit lässt sich auch der Ägypten Level, wo vorher in einigen Bereichen gerade mal 24 FPS geschafft wurden, jetzt ohne Probleme mit 30 FPS + auf der Claw spielen.
Bei 007 First Light bin ich sehr auf die Switch 2 Version gespannt. Der unterschied in dem Spiel zwischen FSR und DLSS sind Welten. Daher gehe ich mal davon aus das die Switch Version, ähnlich wie Indiana Jones, eine deutlich bessere Bildschärfe haben wird und damit dann auch wieder die XBox Series S ausstechen wird, was die Bildqualität betrifft.

I've had an LCD 512gb Steam Deck since about 2023(?) and I've gotten hella use out of it for flights and long road trips. The fact that it's so easy to just set up a game and have it run (most of the time) but with the option to customize it with mods if I want to is the appeal for me

I don't understand people who opt for other handhelds with more power or higher res screens. I don't need that stuff, I have my desktop pc for gaming power and my Steam Deck for portability. 800p 40hz is more than enough for a handheld for me, that's usually what I run my Deck at just for the sake of keeping that battery alive as long as I can

video_title: Steam Deck in 2025 (Still Worth Getting?)
comment_text:
This is a completely disingenuous review really only trying to spread the gospel of the cult of gabism than an actual review to help the normal person make a buying decision.  The oled screen at a $550 price point puts this at the top of the market for someone that wants to play single player games...this is not consolelike...capping framerates and tweaking settings is not something console gamers want to do.  Most of the games in my library have the "not compatible" check.  I've had steam since valve released it along with half life 2 which means i have mostly legacy games that arent controller friendly.  I don't want to see keyboard and mouse button prompts when i boot up portal.  I don't enjoy not having aim assist in battlefield when i do when i remote play my xbox on my ally.

This thing is thin and light, only second to the switch for the all important total weight which cuts down on hand fatigue.  However, remote players like the ps portal are lighter.  The trackpads are useless for most people and they force the sticks and buttons in a bad spot.  I had to look down at the thing for a while when i first got it to find buttons and sticks.  The back buttons are awful.  Theyre right where your fingers are and you can't get a good grip.  This makes shoulder buttons veeeery awkward to hit.  Plus they are terrible membrane actuation and back buttons need to be mechanical...your fingers don't have dexterity like your thumbs.  This thing screams cost cutting.  It also looks awful....definitely designed by a comp sci grad rather than an actual designer.  It looks like what people thought things from the future would look like in 1980.  You're not gonna set this up in a best buy next to an ipad and not have people go ew.

With that said, the screen is great and it plays games.  Steam remote play is better than ps remote play as it supports hdr and high refresh rate.  Neither supports 3d audio like xbox though.  Still a great buy for the right person but not as good as a switch 2.  The fandom around this thing is insufferable.  I fixed your review.  You're welcome

comment_text:
From my perspective: I enjoy PC gaming for modding games. I mean I love trying new mods for older games, and I also wanted to try a handheld.
So, I needed to learn the basics of Linux to run many modding tools. And if at first months it was just bad, then after getting good at it, I stopped using Windows on my other computers.

comment_text: If you can bring your PC to where you are, you can setup Apollo or sunshine and moonlight, that way your pc can just be plugged into the wall, and you game stream from the PC to your SD, fully using your graphics card and PC raw power while still having the benefits of playing on a handheld. This setup as well the deck lasts incredibly wrong since all its doing is olaying a video and passing on the input. It also gets passed all linux limitations as the game is being played on your main PC, and if thats windows, anticheat or anything else does not matter. I have moonlight set up in my house and can play directly on my TV, phone, steamdeck, etc using my pc at my desk.

comment_text: Nice to see a real review of the steam deck instead of a nonstop glaze session.  Probably pound for pound the best PC handheld but it definitely has drawbacks.  For a PC handheld it is quite sleek and efficient, it has better HDR than other PC handhelds, but the controls are pretty awful. The sleeker form factor, better optimization, and DLSS plus having the new handheld boost mode for back compat is just too good.
